additionalTime: 15 minutes dried chile soak plus 15 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Smooth simmered guajillo-arbol salsa for spooning over burrito bites, with roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, garlic, onion, toasted dried chiles, a little vinegar and lime, honey, and a hot-oil finish.
ingredients: 2 dried guajillo chiles, stemmed and seeded
4 dried chile de arbol peppers, stemmed
1/2 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water, plus more as needed
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
instructions: Toast 2 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chiles and 4 stemmed dried chile de arbol peppers in a dry skillet over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not blackened.
Place the toasted chiles in a small bowl, cover with very hot water, and soak for 15 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 3 Roma tomatoes, 2 tomatillos, 1/4 small white onion, and 4 unpeeled garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet or comal over medium-high heat.
Roast for 10 to 14 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatoes and tomatillos soften, the onion chars in spots, and the garlic is browned and soft inside.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for blending and thinning instead.

Add the soaked chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, peeled garlic, 1/2 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1 1/2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon honey, 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to a blender.
Blend on high for 35 to 60 seconds until very smooth.
For the silkiest drip texture, pass the blended salsa through a fine-mesh strainer; for a slightly rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Heat 2 tablespoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat until shimmering.
Carefully pour the blended salsa into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer over medium-low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring often, until the color deepens slightly, the oil looks integrated, and the salsa lightly coats a spoon but still drips easily.
If the salsa gets too thick, whisk in reserved chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time until it is thin enough to drip from a spoon.
Taste and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
Let the salsa rest for 15 minutes before serving.
notes: This salsa is smoother and more chile-forward than the everyday roja, so it works well spooned onto burrito bites.
Four chile de arbol peppers gives a useful medium-hot default. Use 2 for mild, 6 for hot, or 8 for very hot.
Frying the blended salsa in hot oil matters. It makes the dried chile flavor rounder, gives the sauce a light gloss, and softens the sharp vinegar and lime flavor.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water gives a cleaner finished salsa.
Aim for a sauce loose enough to drip from a spoon but thick enough to briefly coat eggs, potatoes, steak, or tortilla.
Do not use scorched chiles. A few blackened spots on roasted vegetables are good, but blackened dried chiles taste bitter.
This salsa works especially well with sausage breakfast burritos, carne asada burritos, eggs, potatoes, tacos, quesadillas, and grilled meats.
title: Simmered Guajillo-Arbol Drip Salsa
